title: Why did this journal retract two 1940s papers by Max Planck?
gist: Science historians found that Naturwissenschaften (now The Science of Nature), a Springer Nature journal, quietly retracted and fully deleted two 1940s papers by Max Planck over apparent copyright/duplication confusion introduced during a 2005 digitization push, revealing how commercial publishers' modern retraction algorithms mishandle historical scientific literature.

themes.theme: Publisher process failure exposed by historians

insights.claim: Springer Nature's journal Naturwissenschaften (now The Science of Nature) retracted two Max Planck papers from the 1940s and removed them entirely, leaving blank pages and empty PDFs with only a note that the articles were withdrawn due to 'article violation,' rather than using the standard RETRACTED-notice overlay the journal applies to other retracted papers.
insights.speaker: Jennifer Ouellette
insights.so_what: Illustrates operational/reputational risk for large scientific publishers when algorithmic retraction processes are applied without human oversight to historical archives; relevant to due diligence on academic publishing platforms.
insights.quote: It has removed the two Planck papers entirely, leaving just a blank page (and empty PDFs) with a brief note saying the articles had been “withdrawn due to article violation.”

insights.claim: The journal's own current editor-in-chief, Suzanne Scarlata of Worcester Polytechnic Institute, was unaware the Planck papers had been retracted until a Science reporter contacted her, and she believes it was likely an algorithmic flagging mistake that should be corrected.
insights.speaker: Jennifer Ouellette
insights.so_what: Signals a governance gap: editorial leadership at a major journal lacks visibility into automated retraction actions taken on its own archive, a red flag for institutional control quality at commercial publishers.
insights.quote: “That's crazy,” she said. “I don't understand why they were flagged. I think it just happened with their algorithm. It's a mistake they should probably rectify.”

insights.claim: Despite the papers being retracted and reduced to empty files, Springer Nature was reportedly still selling the blank PDFs for $39.95 each, according to Science reporter Sam Kean, though Ars's own writer found she could download both empty files for free.
insights.speaker: Jennifer Ouellette
insights.so_what: A minor but telling example of commercial publishers monetizing broken/empty content without quality control, relevant to critiques of academic publishing paywall economics.
insights.quote: Kean claims Springer Nature is still selling the empty PDFs for $39.95 a pop, but I had no trouble downloading both empty files for free, for what it's worth.

themes.theme: Root cause: 2005 digitization and cataloguing ambiguity

insights.claim: Historians Yves Gingras and Mahdi Khelfaoui found metadata showing the DOI records for both retracted Planck papers were created in April 2005, coinciding with the large-scale industry switch to electronic publishing, when journals began integrating historical archives into searchable online systems.
insights.speaker: Jennifer Ouellette
insights.so_what: Pinpoints 2005 digitization as the likely origin point of the error, suggesting other historical papers processed during that same digitization wave across publishers could carry similar undetected retraction/metadata errors worth auditing.
insights.quote: They were able to retrieve metadata showing that the DOI records for both papers had been created in April 2005, coinciding with the large-scale switch to electronic publishing that occurred across most journals.

insights.claim: The historians concluded the retractions likely stemmed from a lawyer at Springer treating early-20th-century publishing practices (where the same lecture/essay was legitimately published across multiple venues) as improper 'duplicate publication' under modern copyright standards.
insights.speaker: Jennifer Ouellette
insights.so_what: Highlights a systemic risk of retroactively applying modern anti-plagiarism/copyright frameworks to historical scientific literature, which could mislabel legitimate historical scholarship as misconduct.
insights.quote: All this clearly suggests that some lawyer at Springer was overshadowing the process and considered these papers as problematic forms of ‘duplicate publications,’

insights.claim: The second retracted paper's removal likely resulted from a cataloguing ambiguity: two different authors (Planck and critic Aloys Muller) published separate papers under the identical title 'Natural Science and the Real External World' in the same journal, which could have confused an algorithmic duplication-detection tool.
insights.speaker: Jennifer Ouellette
insights.so_what: A concrete mechanism showing how naive algorithmic plagiarism detection (matching on title alone) can misfire on legitimate scholarly exchanges, a cautionary data point for any automated content-moderation or duplicate-detection system.
insights.quote: there were two separate papers by different authors in the same journal with identical titles. This would have confused any algorithmic tool used to catch instances of duplication or “self-plagiarism,” for example.

insights.claim: Early 20th-century scientific publishing emphasized wide dissemination of knowledge across a fragmented, multi-language scientific community, so it was normal and accepted for the same lecture or essay to appear as a journal article, a booklet, and in an anthology simultaneously, blurring boundaries that modern duplication standards do not accommodate.
insights.speaker: Jennifer Ouellette
insights.so_what: A reusable framework for evaluating any historical-document dispute in scholarly publishing: judge duplication claims against the publication norms of the era in which the work was produced, not against present-day copyright standards.
insights.quote: The emphasis was on achieving the widest dissemination of knowledge across a fragmented scientific community separated by language and geographical distance, publishing in many different journals.

insights.claim: Modern scientific publishing has shifted to being dominated by large commercial publishing groups more focused on protecting copyright and profit, and duplication/self-plagiarism concerns have become more consequential now because publication counts directly affect hiring, promotion, and research funding decisions.
insights.speaker: Jennifer Ouellette
insights.so_what: Frames the commercialization of academic publishing as the underlying driver of stricter (and sometimes historically inappropriate) enforcement of duplication rules, relevant context for assessing publisher business models and academic-incentive structures.
insights.quote: The scientific enterprise has since evolved to the point where it is dominated by large commercial publishing groups that are much more sensitive to protecting copyrights and turning a profit.

insights.claim: Springer Nature killed an editorial that editor-in-chief Suzanne Scarlata had planned to run addressing the retraction issue, and declined to comment for the original Science article beyond stating that retraction details are confidential.
insights.speaker: Jennifer Ouellette
insights.so_what: Suggests active suppression of internal editorial transparency by the publisher even after the error was flagged externally, a governance red flag relevant to assessing Springer Nature's institutional accountability.
insights.quote: The journal's publisher, Springer Nature, killed an editorial Scarlata planned to run addressing the issue.

insights.claim: Both Planck papers are now in the public domain in most countries, meaning the original copyright-violation rationale for retraction is now moot, yet the historians argue the removal still distorts the historical scientific record and should be reversed.
insights.speaker: Jennifer Ouellette
insights.so_what: Underscores the reputational/legacy risk publishers face by leaving erroneous retractions uncorrected even after the stated legal justification disappears; a call to action for archive integrity that investors in academic-publishing reputational risk should note.
insights.quote: Whoever did it, I don't care,” Gingras told Science. “Just put them [back] in the database. Intellectually, it's not acceptable.”
